0: <title>Green Electricity From Biomass, Part I: Environmental Impacts of Direct Life Cycle Emissions </title>
Sonja Siegl Michael Laaber Peter Holubar
Received: 7 February 2011 / Accepted: 24 May 2011 Published online: 9 June 2011 © Springer Science+Business Media B.V. 2011

Abstract For the evaluation of the environmental impacts of direct life cycle emissions resulting from elec- tricity generation from biomass, various biomass conver- sion technologies (anaerobic digestion [five different plant types], combustion and gasification) are investigated on the basis of data from existing plants in Austria. The envi- ronmental impacts are analyzed in accordance with ISO 14040 for life cycle assessment (LCA). For the impact assessment the CML 2001 base line approach covering 11 impact categories is used. The results show how different life cycle stages dominate the emission profile in various impact categories and that the dominant life cycle stages differ depending on the conversion technology. The dif- ference between the results for the various conversion technologies is up to a factor of 12. In the impact categories of climate change, acidification and eutrophication, direct life cycle emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are considerably higher than from combustion and gasification due to the differences in the nature of the co-products (digestate or ash). The LCA results show potentials for improvement which could achieve emission reductions of between 10 and 50% in certain impact categories. Based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact assessment results, the comparison shows that the ranking is led by anaerobic digestion using mainly energy crops, the com- bustion of wood chips, anaerobic digestion of primarily

4: Currently, the development of various renewable energy technologies and increase in the share of "renewables" in total energy production are the target of European as well as international agreements dealing with energy and cli- mate change. Renewable energy is commonly accepted as a sustainable technology. European Union leaders have reached an agreement that 20% of their nations' energy should be produced from renewable fuels by 2020. At 34%, Austria's target is even higher [1].

Despite the boom in the installation of new plants for green electricity generation, up to now scientific evaluation of the overall environmental effects has been only partially conducted for some environmental impacts or one specific technology. For this evaluation it is important to consider the entire life cycle including the construction and decon- struction of the plant as well as provision of the energy carrier. Therefore the appropriate tool for the comparison of different technologies of electricity generation is the life cycle assessment (LCA).

In Austria the production of biogas in anaerobic diges- tion plants and the utilization of solid biomass in com- bustion (and gasification) plants are the most common conversion technologies used for electricity generation from biomass. For anaerobic digestion LCAs have been conducted by Börjesson and Berglund [2] and Spielmann et al. [3] and for electricity generation from wood chips by Jungmeier et al. [4] or Bauer et al. [5].

Due to the great complexity of the different inputs and mass flows which characterize biomass conversion tech- nologies (land use, residues as substrates, various co- products), the LCA results for electricity generation from biomass depend largely upon the way in which system boundaries are drawn and various reference systems and allocation methods handled.

To be able to present the results of the LCA of green electricity from biomass in a transparent and traceable manner, we have divided them into two parts: part I covers direct life cycle emissions, while part II considers reference systems and questions of allocation.

The present paper aims to provide an estimation of the overall environmental effects of direct life cycle emissions from electricity production in small-scale (up to 5 MWe1) biomass plants. The assessment is based on comprehensive data collected from 34 existing biomass plants operating
under the Green Electricity Act in Austria. The study covers seven different plant configurations and sizes as well as substrate mixes.

For the evaluation of the environmental effects, emis- sions in 11 impact categories including the abiotic deple- tion potential (ADP), global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), ozone depletion potential (ODP), photochemical oxidants creation potential (POCP) and various toxicity potentials (TP) are calculated for all stages of the production chain. The results are used to identify crucial life cycle stages, to reveal potentials for improvement and to compare different conversion technologies.

7: The environmental impacts of electricity production from biomass are determined in accordance with the interna- tional standard ISO 14040:2006 for LCA [6].

LCA is a process for evaluating the environmental burdens or benefits associated with the total life cycle of a product by identifying and quantifying the energy and materials used and waste products released into the environment and for assessing the impacts of the energy and materials used and released into the environment. In accordance with ISO 14040:2006 the entire LCA procedure is divided into four distinct phases: goal and scope definition, inventory anal- ysis, impact assessment and interpretation [6].

The goal of the study is to assess the environmental impacts of direct life cycle emissions from electricity generation in small-scale biomass plants in order to iden- tify crucial life cycle stages for the improvement of overall environmental performance and to compare different con- version technologies.

The biomass conversion technologies covered by this study include the anaerobic digestion of varying compositions of energy crops, manure and organic residues in different plant sizes with two different combined heat and power (CHP) engines as well as the combustion and gasification of wood-chips (see Table 1).
The anaerobic digestion plants investigated in this study consist of the following processes: storage and charging of substrates, anaerobic digestion (fermenter, operated as
Table 1 Description of the types of biomass plants investigated in this study

Abbreviation|Technology|Substrates|Electricity generation|Number of plants investigated AD, EC|Anaerobic digestion|70% energy crops|CHP gas engine|9 AD, M|Anaerobic digestion||CHP gas engine|8 AD, R|Anaerobic digestion|50% organic residues|CHP gas engine|6 AD, PI|Anaerobic digestion|10% energy crops, 70% manure, 20% residues|CHP pilot injection gas engine|3 AD, <50 kW|Anaerobic digestion|10% energy crops, 70% manure, 20% residues|CHP small gas engine Pinst < 50 kWel|4 C, WC|Combustion|100% wood chips|CHP steam turbine|3 G, WC|Gasification|100% wood chips|CHP gas engine|1

continuous stirred tank reactor [CSTR], in most of the cases two stages), storage of digestate (in open or gas-tight closed storage facilities), storage of biogas, utilization of biogas in a CHP plant (gas engine or pilot injection gas engine with electricity generator and heat utilization sys- tem) and the spreading of digestate (splash plate method, trail hose method or injection method). A detailed expla- nation of the technology of anaerobic digestion can be found, for example, in Speece [7] or Balat et al. [8].

The combustion plants investigated in this study consist of the following process steps: storage and charging of wood chips, firing system (biomass combustion in a grate furnace), steam system (water tube boiler and steam delivery system), steam turbine (extraction condensing turbine) with electric generator, feed water and condensate system and flue gas cleaning (multi cyclone and electro- static precipitator). A detailed explanation of combustion technology can be found in Obernberger [9].

The gasification plant under investigation in this study consists of the following process stages: storage and charging of wood chips, gasifier (fast internal circulating fluidized bed [FICFB] gasifier), product gas cleaning sys- tem (gas filter and scrubber), gas engine with electricity generator, heat utilization system and flue gas cleaning (catalyst for CO emission reduction and gas filter for par- ticle separation). A detailed explanation of gasification technology can be found in McKendry [10] and Hofbauer et al. [11].

The gasification plant under investigation in this study consists of the following process stages: storage and charging of wood chips, gasifier (fast internal circulating fluidized bed [FICFB] gasifier), product gas cleaning sys- tem (gas filter and scrubber), gas engine with electricity generator, heat utilization system and flue gas cleaning (catalyst for CO emission reduction and gas filter for par- ticle separation). A detailed explanation of gasification technology can be found in McKendry [10] and Hofbauer et al. [11].
11: The main life cycle stages covering direct life cycle emis- sions discussed in part I of the paper are the "production of biomass", "transport to the plant", "operation of the bio- mass plant", "utilization or disposal of the co-products" and the "construction and future demolition of the infra- structure". A detailed description of the processes consid- ered within the system boundary is provided in Fig. 1.
12: In addition to the main product, electricity, biomass sys- tems produce various amounts of additional co-products and have functions that yield quantifiable benefits (e.g. heat, digestate, ash, disposal of residues). The process of assigning to each of the functions only those environmental burdens associated with that particular function is called allocation (Azapagic and Clift [12]). The allocation pro- cedure recommended by the ISO standard [6] and adopted in this study is the system expansion method (or substitu- tion approach). In this approach the system boundaries are expanded to include the additional functions related to the co-products. For example, if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= reference system), the environmental load of the avoi- ded heat production (= credit) may be subtracted. A prac- tical problem of the substitution approach is the selection of the reference systems [13]. As already stated by Jung- meier et al. [4] and Singh et al. [14], allocation has to be given special attention in the LCAs of bioenergy because results depend heavily on the allocation method used. In the present study, so-called "reference systems" (see light- collared boxes in Fig. 1) are defined for all of the inputs used and co-products produced by the biomass systems in order to assure that the functions fulfilled by each tech- nology are equal. Because of the comprehensiveness of this topic, these reference systems are discussed in part II of this paper.
13: The life cycle inventory analysis (LCI) covers the collec- tion of data and methods of calculation for the quantifica- tion of relevant input- and output flows for a given product system.

15: In the present study, the following biomass substrates were considered: energy crops, manure, organic residues and wood chips, which are characterized in Table 2. As addressed in previous studies dealing with the LCA of biomass systems [2, 3, 27, 28], manure and organic resi- dues are considered to be by- or waste products of other systems and therefore their production is not included in this LCA. However, energy crops and wood chips are produced exclusively for biomass plants and consequently their production is considered.

For calculation of the inputs and emissions resulting from the production of energy crops, the annual field work pro- cesses needed [29] as well as the amount of seed, plant protecting agents and synthetic fertilizer used were collected in interviews with plant operators and combined with the appropriate ecoinvent datasets [17]. The different energy

crops considered, the necessary field work processes as well as minimum and maximum yields are provided in Table 3. For calculation of the inputs and emissions resulting from the production of wood chips, various supply chains were considered. They are characterized by the degree of mechanization (motor manual or mechanized) of the har- vesting process and different types of wood. The assump- tions made for fuel consumption, productivity and machine use in combination with the corresponding ecoinvent datasets used are provided in Table 4. According to the information collected in interviews with plant operators, the wood chips for the combustion, wood chip (C, WC) plant type were produced in the following supply chains: 29% motor manual, energy wood; 2% mechanized, energy wood; 22% motor manual, residual felling wood and 46% mechanized, residual felling wood. For the gasification, wood chip (G, WC) plant type 20% of wood chip pro- duction was motor manual from energy wood and 80% mechanized from round wood.

For calculation of inputs and emissions resulting from the transport of the different substrates to the biomass plants, the ecoinvent datasets "transport, tractor and trailer", "transport, lorry 16t" and "transport, lorry 32t" were used [17]. The amounts of each substrate were collected in interviews with plant operators. The average transport

21: The life cycle stage "operation of the biomass plant" consists of inputs to and emissions from the handling of substrates at the plant (e.g. ensilage of energy crops), the production and utilization of auxiliary materials (e.g. cat- alyst, bed material, lubricating and pilot oil) as well as the energy demand for operation (e.g. fuel for charging, elec- tricity for mixing, etc.) and direct emissions from the combustion process.

Gaseous emissions of NOx, NMVOC, CO, CH4, SO2 and particulates from the combustion of the biomass or product gas (anaerobic digestion, gasification) were con- sidered. Calculation methods are explained in Table 5. CO2 emissions arising directly from the combustion of the biomass or product gas were regarded as climate neutral
according to Cherubini et al. [39] and therefore not con- sidered for the inventory.
22: <header>Co-Product Utilization/Disposal </header>

In the case of anaerobic digestion, the main co-product is the so-called digestate, which is usually stored for a specified amount of time (regulated by national laws) and subsequently spread on fields as a fertilizer. In the case of combustion and gasification, further co-products include various fractions of ash, which are usually disposed in landfills. Causing no further emissions, the co-product heat, which is produced in the CHP plant simultaneously with electricity can be classified as "good", whereas the co- products digestate and ash are classified as residues, whose utilization/disposal causes environmental impacts which have to be considered within the system boundary of the biomass system.

For consideration of the environmental impacts from the disposal of ash in a landfill as well as the transport to the landfill, corresponding ecoinvent datasets [17] were used.

According to Edelmann et al. [40], gaseous emissions (CH4, N2O and NH3) from the storage and spreading of the digestate are responsible for a considerable part of the total environmental impact of electricity generation from bio- gas. As there were no measurement results available for these emissions, they were calculated using emission fac- tors derived from the literature.

For calculation of the methane emissions from open storage facilities, the fraction of organic dry matter (ODM), which is degraded in open storage facilities, was calculated via degradation curves dependent on the hydraulic reten- tion time.
(AODM fraction of ODM degraded after a period of d days in [%]).

For consideration of the environmental impacts from the disposal of ash in a landfill as well as the transport to the landfill, corresponding ecoinvent datasets [17] were used.

According to Edelmann et al. [40], gaseous emissions (CH4, N2O and NH3) from the storage and spreading of the digestate are responsible for a considerable part of the total environmental impact of electricity generation from bio- gas. As there were no measurement results available for these emissions, they were calculated using emission fac- tors derived from the literature.

For calculation of the methane emissions from open storage facilities, the fraction of organic dry matter (ODM), which is degraded in open storage facilities, was calculated via degradation curves dependent on the hydraulic reten- tion time.
(AODM fraction of ODM degraded after a period of d days in [%]).
The amount of methane produced was calculated from AODM using the specific methane yield of each plant
24: <header>Co-Product Utilization/Disposal </header>

In the case of anaerobic digestion, the main co-product is the so-called digestate, which is usually stored for a specified amount of time (regulated by national laws) and subsequently spread on fields as a fertilizer. In the case of combustion and gasification, further co-products include various fractions of ash, which are usually disposed in landfills. Causing no further emissions, the co-product heat, which is produced in the CHP plant simultaneously with electricity can be classified as "good", whereas the co- products digestate and ash are classified as residues, whose utilization/disposal causes environmental impacts which have to be considered within the system boundary of the biomass system.

According to Edelmann et al. [40], gaseous emissions (CH4, N2O and NH3) from the storage and spreading of the digestate are responsible for a considerable part of the total environmental impact of electricity generation from bio- gas. As there were no measurement results available for these emissions, they were calculated using emission fac- tors derived from the literature.

For calculation of the methane emissions from open storage facilities, the fraction of organic dry matter (ODM), which is degraded in open storage facilities, was calculated via degradation curves dependent on the hydraulic reten- tion time.
(AODM fraction of ODM degraded after a period of d days in [%]).
The amount of methane produced was calculated from AODM using the specific methane yield of each plant
26: <header>Co-Product Utilization/Disposal </header>

In the case of anaerobic digestion, the main co-product is the so-called digestate, which is usually stored for a specified amount of time (regulated by national laws) and subsequently spread on fields as a fertilizer. In the case of combustion and gasification, further co-products include various fractions of ash, which are usually disposed in landfills. Causing no further emissions, the co-product heat, which is produced in the CHP plant simultaneously with electricity can be classified as "good", whereas the co- products digestate and ash are classified as residues, whose utilization/disposal causes environmental impacts which have to be considered within the system boundary of the biomass system.

For consideration of the environmental impacts from the disposal of ash in a landfill as well as the transport to the landfill, corresponding ecoinvent datasets [17] were used.

According to Edelmann et al. [40], gaseous emissions (CH4, N2O and NH3) from the storage and spreading of the digestate are responsible for a considerable part of the total environmental impact of electricity generation from bio- gas. As there were no measurement results available for these emissions, they were calculated using emission fac- tors derived from the literature.

For calculation of the methane emissions from open storage facilities, the fraction of organic dry matter (ODM), which is degraded in open storage facilities, was calculated via degradation curves dependent on the hydraulic reten- tion time.
(AODM fraction of ODM degraded after a period of d days in [%]).
The amount of methane produced was calculated from AODM using the specific methane yield of each plant
29: <header>Co-Product Utilization/Disposal </header>

In the case of anaerobic digestion, the main co-product is the so-called digestate, which is usually stored for a specified amount of time (regulated by national laws) and subsequently spread on fields as a fertilizer. In the case of combustion and gasification, further co-products include various fractions of ash, which are usually disposed in landfills. Causing no further emissions, the co-product heat, which is produced in the CHP plant simultaneously with electricity can be classified as "good", whereas the co- products digestate and ash are classified as residues, whose utilization/disposal causes environmental impacts which have to be considered within the system boundary of the biomass system.

In the case of anaerobic digestion, the main co-product is the so-called digestate, which is usually stored for a specified amount of time (regulated by national laws) and subsequently spread on fields as a fertilizer. In the case of combustion and gasification, further co-products include various fractions of ash, which are usually disposed in landfills. Causing no further emissions, the co-product heat, which is produced in the CHP plant simultaneously with electricity can be classified as "good", whereas the co- products digestate and ash are classified as residues, whose utilization/disposal causes environmental impacts which have to be considered within the system boundary of the biomass system.

For consideration of the environmental impacts from the disposal of ash in a landfill as well as the transport to the landfill, corresponding ecoinvent datasets [17] were used.

According to Edelmann et al. [40], gaseous emissions (CH4, N2O and NH3) from the storage and spreading of the digestate are responsible for a considerable part of the total environmental impact of electricity generation from bio- gas. As there were no measurement results available for these emissions, they were calculated using emission fac- tors derived from the literature.

For calculation of the methane emissions from open storage facilities, the fraction of organic dry matter (ODM), which is degraded in open storage facilities, was calculated via degradation curves dependent on the hydraulic reten- tion time.
(AODM fraction of ODM degraded after a period of d days in [%]).
The amount of methane produced was calculated from AODM using the specific methane yield of each plant

[Nm³ CH4 kg ODM], reduced by a factor of 51% [41, 42] due to lower temperatures and exposure to oxygen in the open storage facility. According to this model of calculation, 0.5-3.64% of ODM fed into the system is degraded in the open storage facility, which causes methane emissions ranging from 0.5 to 1.5% of the methane produced in the plants. The emis- sion factors used in other studies on this subject range from 1 to 15% [43].

The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. Most of the data was available for the storage and spreading of liquid manure. The average emission factors used are provided in Table 6. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

The following impact categories were used: climate change (GWP), AP, EP, POCP (= smog), ADP, ODP, land use competition (LUC) and various TPs. |Results "Due to physical conditions, ammonia emissions from the spreading of digestate essentially depend on the application technique used [19, 25, 40, 59]. When the ""trail hose"" application technique is used instead of the ""splash plate method"" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [19, 25, 40, 59, 60]. With the ""injection (closed slot)"" application technique NH3 emissions are assumed to be reduced by 86%, while N2O emissions are assumed to be increased by a factor of 2.95 [25, 40, 49, 55, 59, 60]. Infrastructure The inputs and emissions resulting from the production, transportation and future disposal of construction materials needed for the construction of the biomass-plants are accounted for in the life cycle stage ""infrastructure"". Data on the amount of concrete, bricks, copper, aluminium, zinc, timber, steel, sheet, plastics, gravel and bitumen was col- lected in personal interviews with plant operators and estimated from data describing the reactor design. Appro- priate ecoinvent datasets [17] were available for all the construction materials used. A plant life time of 13 years and one CHP engine change were assumed. Summary of Input Parameters"|"Life Cycle Impact Assessment Results In the following paragraphs and figures results for the different impact categories, life cycle stages and plant types are presented. More detailed data is available from the authors on request. Global Warming Potential (GWP) The GWP describes the emissions of different greenhouse gases into the air expressed in kg CO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are CO2 (CF = 1), CO (CF = 1.53), CH4 (CF = 23) and N2O (CF = 296) [61]. The results are presented in Fig. 2. For the anaerobic digestion plants emissions per kWh electricity are gener- ally higher (0.25-0.55 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1 than for com- bustion and gasification plants (0.08 and 0.09 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1 For anaerobic digestion plants the life cycle stages ""operation of the plant"" and ""co-product utilization"" are responsible for approx. 75% of total greenhouse gas emissions. The substances responsible for the GWP of these life cycle stages are CH4 emissions from the CHP and the storage of the digestate as well as N2O emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For the anaer- obic digestion plants using mainly residues the contribution of ""transport"" (0.11 kg CO2-eq. kWh is comparatively higher than for other anaerobic digestion plant types due to the fact that the average transport distance is higher" Table 7 provides an overview of the most important input parameters calculated in the LCI. Life Cycle Impact Assessment|"(36.5 km) than, for example, for the plant type anaerobic digestion, energy crops (AD, EC) at 4.7 km. For small anaerobic digestion plants (CHP engine <50 kWe1), the contribution of ""infrastructure"" to the total results is higher than for other anaerobic digestion plants" "In the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) phase of an LCA, the information gathered in the inventory is con- densed into a few environmental parameters to facilitate communication and/or decision-making support. In this paper, the internationally well-known mid-point method ""CML 2001"" developed by Guinée et al. [61] was used. It covers all emission- and resource-related impacts for which practical and acceptable characterization meth- ods are available. It was chosen because of its scientific"|due to the fact that the energetic and material input needed for their construction is proportionally higher than for bigger anaerobic digestion plants and that small plants often use a steel fermenter, which needs more resource input than a fermenter made of concrete. Furthermore, small CHP engines often show a comparatively low elec- trical efficiency (29% compared to 36.6% for AD, EC), which makes the results per kWh electricity higher in all impact categories.

The differences between the GWP of the life cycle stage "co-product utilization" for the various plant types arise from variations in the amount of digestate spread per ha, the concentration of TAN in the digestate, the storage conditions, the application technique as well as the elec- trical efficiency.

For combustion plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass" (57%, 0.04 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1), followed by its "transport" (29%, 0.02 kg CO2- eq. kWh-1 The main substance resulting in the GWP is CO2 from the combustion of fossil fuels.

For the gasification plant investigated, the dominant life cycle stage is the "operation of the plant" (52%, 0.05 kg CO2-eq. kWh due to the comparable high demand of operating resources, followed by the "production of bio- mass" (34%, 0.03 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1).
Acidification and Eutrophication Potential (AP and EP)

For the AP, the emissions of acidifying substances into the air are summed up and expressed in SO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 1.88), NO (CF = 0.7) and SO2 (CF = 1). The EP covers emissions of nutrients into air, water and soil, which cause over-fertil- ization, expressed in PO4 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 0.35) and NO (CF = 0.13). As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 2. They show that emissions from the "operation of the plant" are on the same scale for all types of biomass plants (0.0014 to 0.0024 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 and 0.0002 to 0.0005 kg PO4- eq. kWh-superscript(1) The substance which causes acidification and

Fig. 2 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
over-fertilization is the NOx from the combustion of the fuel.

While this life cycle stage is dominant for combustion and gasification plants, causing around 65% of the total AP and EP, the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plants is "co-product utilization", which is responsible for 67-90% of their acidifying and over-fer- tilizing emissions. The differences between the various types of anaerobic digestion plants for this life cycle stage (0.0036 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 for AD, EC and 0.0209 kg SO2-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) for AD, PI) arise from varying amounts of digestate spread per ha (from 10 to 75 various con- centrations of TAN as well as the various application techniques applied. The substance responsible for the AP and EP in this life cycle stage is ammonia. Improvement potentials for these impact categories such as additional flue gas cleaning or alternative digestate application tech- niques are discussed later on.

In this paper, the internationally well-known mid-point method ""CML 2001"" developed by Guinée et al. [61] was used. It covers all emission- and resource-related impacts for which practical and acceptable characterization meth- ods are available. Table 7 Summary of most important input parameters for the different biomass plant types investigated calculated in the life cycle inventory (mean values)

AD anaerobic digestion; C combustion; G gasification; EC energy crops; M manure; R organic residues; PI pilot injection gas engine; WC wood chips; op operation of plant; HRT hydraulic retention time; st storage of digestate; sp spreading of digestate; dig digestate

The differences between the GWP of the life cycle stage "co-product utilization" for the various plant types arise from variations in the amount of digestate spread per ha, the concentration of TAN in the digestate, the storage conditions, the application technique as well as the elec- trical efficiency.

For combustion plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass" (57%, 0.04 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1), followed by its "transport" (29%, 0.02 kg CO2- eq. kWh-1 The main substance resulting in the GWP is CO2 from the combustion of fossil fuels.

For the gasification plant investigated, the dominant life cycle stage is the "operation of the plant" (52%, 0.05 kg CO2-eq. kWh due to the comparable high demand of operating resources, followed by the "production of bio- mass" (34%, 0.03 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1).
Acidification and Eutrophication Potential (AP and EP)

For the AP, the emissions of acidifying substances into the air are summed up and expressed in SO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 1.88), NO (CF = 0.7) and SO2 (CF = 1). The EP covers emissions of nutrients into air, water and soil, which cause over-fertil- ization, expressed in PO4 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 0.35) and NO (CF = 0.13). As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 2. They show that emissions from the "operation of the plant" are on the same scale for all types of biomass plants (0.0014 to 0.0024 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 and 0.0002 to 0.0005 kg PO4- eq. kWh-superscript(1) The substance which causes acidification and

over-fertilization is the NOx from the combustion of the fuel.

While this life cycle stage is dominant for combustion and gasification plants, causing around 65% of the total AP and EP, the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plants is "co-product utilization", which is responsible for 67-90% of their acidifying and over-fer- tilizing emissions. The differences between the various types of anaerobic digestion plants for this life cycle stage (0.0036 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 for AD, EC and 0.0209 kg SO2-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) for AD, PI) arise from varying amounts of digestate spread per ha (from 10 to 75 various con- centrations of TAN as well as the various application techniques applied. The substance responsible for the AP and EP in this life cycle stage is ammonia. Improvement potentials for these impact categories such as additional flue gas cleaning or alternative digestate application tech- niques are discussed later on.

According to this model of calculation, 0.5-3.64% of ODM fed into the system is degraded in the open storage facility, which causes methane emissions ranging from 0.5 to 1.5% of the methane produced in the plants. The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. Most of the data was available for the storage and spreading of liquid manure. The average emission factors used are provided in Table 6. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

When the ""trail hose"" application technique is used instead of the ""splash plate method"" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [19, 25, 40, 59, 60]. With the ""injection (closed slot)"" application technique NH3 emissions are assumed to be reduced by 86%, while N2O emissions are assumed to be increased by a factor of 2.95 [25, 40, 49, 55, 59, 60]. Infrastructure The inputs and emissions resulting from the production, transportation and future disposal of construction materials needed for the construction of the biomass-plants are accounted for in the life cycle stage ""infrastructure"". Data on the amount of concrete, bricks, copper, aluminium, zinc, timber, steel, sheet, plastics, gravel and bitumen was col- lected in personal interviews with plant operators and estimated from data describing the reactor design. Appro- priate ecoinvent datasets [17] were available for all the construction materials used. A plant life time of 13 years and one CHP engine change were assumed. [51]; Külling et al. [52]; Schimpl [53]; Amon et al. [47]; Kryvoruchko [21]; Olesen et al. [54]; Amon et al. [23]; Clemens et al. [24] d Mean value from Edelmann et al. [Nm³ CH4 kg ODM], reduced by a factor of 51% [41, 42] due to lower temperatures and exposure to oxygen in the open storage facility. According to this model of calculation, 0.5-3.64% of ODM fed into the system is degraded in the open storage facility, which causes methane emissions ranging from 0.5 to 1.5% of the methane produced in the plants. The emis- sion factors used in other studies on this subject range from 1 to 15% [43].

The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. Most of the data was available for the storage and spreading of liquid manure. The average emission factors used are provided in Table 6. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

|basis, the wide range of impact categories available and considerable popularity within the life cycle community. The following impact categories were used: climate change (GWP), AP, EP, POCP (= smog), ADP, ODP, land use competition (LUC) and various TPs. |Results "Due to physical conditions, ammonia emissions from the spreading of digestate essentially depend on the application technique used [19, 25, 40, 59]. When the ""trail hose"" application technique is used instead of the ""splash plate method"" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [19, 25, 40, 59, 60]. With the ""injection (closed slot)"" application technique NH3 emissions are assumed to be reduced by 86%, while N2O emissions are assumed to be increased by a factor of 2.95 [25, 40, 49, 55, 59, 60]. Infrastructure The inputs and emissions resulting from the production, transportation and future disposal of construction materials needed for the construction of the biomass-plants are accounted for in the life cycle stage ""infrastructure"". Data on the amount of concrete, bricks, copper, aluminium, zinc, timber, steel, sheet, plastics, gravel and bitumen was col- lected in personal interviews with plant operators and estimated from data describing the reactor design. Appro- priate ecoinvent datasets [17] were available for all the construction materials used. A plant life time of 13 years and one CHP engine change were assumed. Summary of Input Parameters"|"Life Cycle Impact Assessment Results In the following paragraphs and figures results for the different impact categories, life cycle stages and plant types are presented. More detailed data is available from the authors on request. Global Warming Potential (GWP) The GWP describes the emissions of different greenhouse gases into the air expressed in kg CO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are CO2 (CF = 1), CO (CF = 1.53), CH4 (CF = 23) and N2O (CF = 296) [61]. The results are presented in Fig. 2. For the anaerobic digestion plants emissions per kWh electricity are gener- ally higher (0.25-0.55 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1 than for com- bustion and gasification plants (0.08 and 0.09 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1 For anaerobic digestion plants the life cycle stages ""operation of the plant"" and ""co-product utilization"" are responsible for approx. 75% of total greenhouse gas emissions. The substances responsible for the GWP of these life cycle stages are CH4 emissions from the CHP and the storage of the digestate as well as N2O emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For the anaer- obic digestion plants using mainly residues the contribution of ""transport"" (0.11 kg CO2-eq. kWh is comparatively higher than for other anaerobic digestion plant types due to the fact that the average transport distance is higher" Table 7 provides an overview of the most important input parameters calculated in the LCI. Life Cycle Impact Assessment|"(36.5 km) than, for example, for the plant type anaerobic digestion, energy crops (AD, EC) at 4.7 km. For small anaerobic digestion plants (CHP engine <50 kWe1), the contribution of ""infrastructure"" to the total results is higher than for other anaerobic digestion plants" "In the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) phase of an LCA, the information gathered in the inventory is con- densed into a few environmental parameters to facilitate communication and/or decision-making support. In this paper, the internationally well-known mid-point method ""CML 2001"" developed by Guinée et al. [61] was used. It covers all emission- and resource-related impacts for which practical and acceptable characterization meth- ods are available. It was chosen because of its scientific"|due to the fact that the energetic and material input needed for their construction is proportionally higher than for bigger anaerobic digestion plants and that small plants often use a steel fermenter, which needs more resource input than a fermenter made of concrete. Furthermore, small CHP engines often show a comparatively low elec- trical efficiency (29% compared to 36.6% for AD, EC), which makes the results per kWh electricity higher in all impact categories.

AD anaerobic digestion; C combustion; G gasification; EC energy crops; M manure; R organic residues; PI pilot injection gas engine; WC wood chips; op operation of plant; HRT hydraulic retention time; st storage of digestate; sp spreading of digestate; dig digestate

The differences between the GWP of the life cycle stage "co-product utilization" for the various plant types arise from variations in the amount of digestate spread per ha, the concentration of TAN in the digestate, the storage conditions, the application technique as well as the elec- trical efficiency.

For combustion plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass" (57%, 0.04 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1), followed by its "transport" (29%, 0.02 kg CO2- eq. kWh-1 The main substance resulting in the GWP is CO2 from the combustion of fossil fuels.

For the gasification plant investigated, the dominant life cycle stage is the "operation of the plant" (52%, 0.05 kg CO2-eq. kWh due to the comparable high demand of operating resources, followed by the "production of bio- mass" (34%, 0.03 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1).
Acidification and Eutrophication Potential (AP and EP)

For the AP, the emissions of acidifying substances into the air are summed up and expressed in SO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 1.88), NO (CF = 0.7) and SO2 (CF = 1). The EP covers emissions of nutrients into air, water and soil, which cause over-fertil- ization, expressed in PO4 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 0.35) and NO (CF = 0.13). As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 2. They show that emissions from the "operation of the plant" are on the same scale for all types of biomass plants (0.0014 to 0.0024 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 and 0.0002 to 0.0005 kg PO4- eq. kWh-superscript(1) The substance which causes acidification and
biomass production
transport
operation plant

infrastructure
GWP CO2-eq. kWh*1

Fig. 2 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
over-fertilization is the NOx from the combustion of the fuel.

While this life cycle stage is dominant for combustion and gasification plants, causing around 65% of the total AP and EP, the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plants is "co-product utilization", which is responsible for 67-90% of their acidifying and over-fer- tilizing emissions. The differences between the various types of anaerobic digestion plants for this life cycle stage (0.0036 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 for AD, EC and 0.0209 kg SO2-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) for AD, PI) arise from varying amounts of digestate spread per ha (from 10 to 75 various con- centrations of TAN as well as the various application techniques applied. The substance responsible for the AP and EP in this life cycle stage is ammonia. Improvement potentials for these impact categories such as additional flue gas cleaning or alternative digestate application tech- niques are discussed later on.

The differences between the GWP of the life cycle stage "co-product utilization" for the various plant types arise from variations in the amount of digestate spread per ha, the concentration of TAN in the digestate, the storage conditions, the application technique as well as the elec- trical efficiency.

For combustion plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass" (57%, 0.04 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1), followed by its "transport" (29%, 0.02 kg CO2- eq. kWh-1 The main substance resulting in the GWP is CO2 from the combustion of fossil fuels.

For the gasification plant investigated, the dominant life cycle stage is the "operation of the plant" (52%, 0.05 kg CO2-eq. kWh due to the comparable high demand of operating resources, followed by the "production of bio- mass" (34%, 0.03 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1).
Acidification and Eutrophication Potential (AP and EP)

For the AP, the emissions of acidifying substances into the air are summed up and expressed in SO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 1.88), NO (CF = 0.7) and SO2 (CF = 1). The EP covers emissions of nutrients into air, water and soil, which cause over-fertil- ization, expressed in PO4 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 0.35) and NO (CF = 0.13). As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 2. They show that emissions from the "operation of the plant" are on the same scale for all types of biomass plants (0.0014 to 0.0024 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 and 0.0002 to 0.0005 kg PO4- eq. kWh-superscript(1) The substance which causes acidification and
biomass production

operation plant

GWP CO2-eq. kWh*1

Fig. 2 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
over-fertilization is the NOx from the combustion of the fuel.

While this life cycle stage is dominant for combustion and gasification plants, causing around 65% of the total AP and EP, the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plants is "co-product utilization", which is responsible for 67-90% of their acidifying and over-fer- tilizing emissions. The differences between the various types of anaerobic digestion plants for this life cycle stage (0.0036 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 for AD, EC and 0.0209 kg SO2-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) for AD, PI) arise from varying amounts of digestate spread per ha (from 10 to 75 various con- centrations of TAN as well as the various application techniques applied. The substance responsible for the AP and EP in this life cycle stage is ammonia. Improvement potentials for these impact categories such as additional flue gas cleaning or alternative digestate application tech- niques are discussed later on.

According to this model of calculation, 0.5-3.64% of ODM fed into the system is degraded in the open storage facility, which causes methane emissions ranging from 0.5 to 1.5% of the methane produced in the plants. The emis- sion factors used in other studies on this subject range from 1 to 15% [43].

The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. Most of the data was available for the storage and spreading of liquid manure. The average emission factors used are provided in Table 6. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

When the ""trail hose"" application technique is used instead of the ""splash plate method"" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [19, 25, 40, 59, 60]. With the ""injection (closed slot)"" application technique NH3 emissions are assumed to be reduced by 86%, while N2O emissions are assumed to be increased by a factor of 2.95 [25, 40, 49, 55, 59, 60]. Infrastructure The inputs and emissions resulting from the production, transportation and future disposal of construction materials needed for the construction of the biomass-plants are accounted for in the life cycle stage ""infrastructure"". Data on the amount of concrete, bricks, copper, aluminium, zinc, timber, steel, sheet, plastics, gravel and bitumen was col- lected in personal interviews with plant operators and estimated from data describing the reactor design. Appro- priate ecoinvent datasets [17] were available for all the construction materials used. A plant life time of 13 years and one CHP engine change were assumed. The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. Most of the data was available for the storage and spreading of liquid manure. The average emission factors used are provided in Table 6. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

|basis, the wide range of impact categories available and considerable popularity within the life cycle community. When the ""trail hose"" application technique is used instead of the ""splash plate method"" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [19, 25, 40, 59, 60]. With the ""injection (closed slot)"" application technique NH3 emissions are assumed to be reduced by 86%, while N2O emissions are assumed to be increased by a factor of 2.95 [25, 40, 49, 55, 59, 60]. Infrastructure The inputs and emissions resulting from the production, transportation and future disposal of construction materials needed for the construction of the biomass-plants are accounted for in the life cycle stage ""infrastructure"". AD anaerobic digestion; C combustion; G gasification; EC energy crops; M manure; R organic residues; PI pilot injection gas engine; WC wood chips; op operation of plant; HRT hydraulic retention time; st storage of digestate; sp spreading of digestate; dig digestate

The differences between the GWP of the life cycle stage "co-product utilization" for the various plant types arise from variations in the amount of digestate spread per ha, the concentration of TAN in the digestate, the storage conditions, the application technique as well as the elec- trical efficiency.

For combustion plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass" (57%, 0.04 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1), followed by its "transport" (29%, 0.02 kg CO2- eq. kWh-1 The main substance resulting in the GWP is CO2 from the combustion of fossil fuels.

For the gasification plant investigated, the dominant life cycle stage is the "operation of the plant" (52%, 0.05 kg CO2-eq. kWh due to the comparable high demand of operating resources, followed by the "production of bio- mass" (34%, 0.03 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1).
Acidification and Eutrophication Potential (AP and EP)

For the AP, the emissions of acidifying substances into the air are summed up and expressed in SO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 1.88), NO (CF = 0.7) and SO2 (CF = 1). The EP covers emissions of nutrients into air, water and soil, which cause over-fertil- ization, expressed in PO4 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 0.35) and NO (CF = 0.13). As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 2. They show that emissions from the "operation of the plant" are on the same scale for all types of biomass plants (0.0014 to 0.0024 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 and 0.0002 to 0.0005 kg PO4- eq. kWh-superscript(1) The substance which causes acidification and

over-fertilization is the NOx from the combustion of the fuel.

While this life cycle stage is dominant for combustion and gasification plants, causing around 65% of the total AP and EP, the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plants is "co-product utilization", which is responsible for 67-90% of their acidifying and over-fer- tilizing emissions. The differences between the various types of anaerobic digestion plants for this life cycle stage (0.0036 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 for AD, EC and 0.0209 kg SO2-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) for AD, PI) arise from varying amounts of digestate spread per ha (from 10 to 75 various con- centrations of TAN as well as the various application techniques applied. The substance responsible for the AP and EP in this life cycle stage is ammonia. Improvement potentials for these impact categories such as additional flue gas cleaning or alternative digestate application tech- niques are discussed later on.

While this life cycle stage is dominant for combustion and gasification plants, causing around 65% of the total AP and EP, the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plants is "co-product utilization", which is responsible for 67-90% of their acidifying and over-fer- tilizing emissions. The differences between the various types of anaerobic digestion plants for this life cycle stage (0.0036 kg SO2-eq. kWh-1 for AD, EC and 0.0209 kg SO2-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) for AD, PI) arise from varying amounts of digestate spread per ha (from 10 to 75 various con- centrations of TAN as well as the various application techniques applied. According to this model of calculation, 0.5-3.64% of ODM fed into the system is degraded in the open storage facility, which causes methane emissions ranging from 0.5 to 1.5% of the methane produced in the plants. The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. Most of the data was available for the storage and spreading of liquid manure. The average emission factors used are provided in Table 6. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

When the ""trail hose"" application technique is used instead of the ""splash plate method"" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [19, 25, 40, 59, 60]. With the ""injection (closed slot)"" application technique NH3 emissions are assumed to be reduced by 86%, while N2O emissions are assumed to be increased by a factor of 2.95 [25, 40, 49, 55, 59, 60]. Infrastructure The inputs and emissions resulting from the production, transportation and future disposal of construction materials needed for the construction of the biomass-plants are accounted for in the life cycle stage ""infrastructure"". Data on the amount of concrete, bricks, copper, aluminium, zinc, timber, steel, sheet, plastics, gravel and bitumen was col- lected in personal interviews with plant operators and estimated from data describing the reactor design. Appro- priate ecoinvent datasets [17] were available for all the construction materials used. A plant life time of 13 years and one CHP engine change were assumed. The differences between the GWP of the life cycle stage "co-product utilization" for the various plant types arise from variations in the amount of digestate spread per ha, the concentration of TAN in the digestate, the storage conditions, the application technique as well as the elec- trical efficiency.

For combustion plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass" (57%, 0.04 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1), followed by its "transport" (29%, 0.02 kg CO2- eq. kWh-1 The main substance resulting in the GWP is CO2 from the combustion of fossil fuels.

For the gasification plant investigated, the dominant life cycle stage is the "operation of the plant" (52%, 0.05 kg CO2-eq. kWh due to the comparable high demand of operating resources, followed by the "production of bio- mass" (34%, 0.03 kg CO2-eq. kWh-1).
Acidification and Eutrophication Potential (AP and EP)

For the AP, the emissions of acidifying substances into the air are summed up and expressed in SO2 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 1.88), NO (CF = 0.7) and SO2 (CF = 1). The EP covers emissions of nutrients into air, water and soil, which cause over-fertil- ization, expressed in PO4 equivalents. Most important for this study are NH3 (CF = 0.35) and NO (CF = 0.13). As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together.

The emission factors for NH3 and N2O emissions from the storage of the digestate as well as NH3, CH4 and N2O emissions from the spreading of the digestate were derived from different sources of literature. The data for the storage and spreading of liquid manure was modified for two different digestate compositions in the following way:

<list>- NH3 emissions from the storage of digestate are 40% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and total ammoniacal nitrogen [TAN] of the digestate; Jäkel et al. [57], Ortenbald [58] and Edelmann et al. [40]). 
- N2O emissions from the storage of digestate are 26% higher than from the storage of untreated liquid manure (due to a higher TAN; Sommer [51]). 
- NH3 emissions from the spreading of digestate are 10% higher than from the spreading of untreated liquid manure (due to the higher pH and TAN; Edelmann et al. [40]).</list>

The results are illustrated in Fig. 2. Emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are up to five times higher than for combustion and gasification plants (3.2 X in comparison to 1.9 X 10- kg C2H4-eq. kWh-1). For anaerobic digestion plants, the main sources are carbon monoxide and methane emissions from the combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine. Again, for the anaerobic digestion plant type using residues, the high contribution of the "transport" stage (20%) to the total results stands out, and for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a CHP <50 kW the compara- tively higher contribution of the "infrastructure" stage to the total results stands out.
For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).

Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the
Table 8 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of calculated emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel in %
Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.

For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).
For the anaerobic digestion in small plants (<50 kWe1), the total result is 0.0008 kg Sb.-eq. kWh- (ADP) and 1:10 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP), and the dominant

For the anaerobic digestion of primarily residues, the total result is approx. 0.0011 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) and 2:10-8 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) (ODP) and the dominant life cycle stage is the "transport" of substrates to the plant.

Electricity production in anaerobic digestion plants using a pilot injection gas engine shows the highest ADP/ ODP of all of the plants investigated (0.0026 kg Sb.-eq. kWh -1[ADP] and 5:10-8 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh [ODP]) due to the demand of pilot oil. This impact category deals with the loss of land as a resource in the sense of it being temporarily unavailable [61]. The unit of the indicator result is m² a-1

The results are illustrated in Fig. 3. The main life cycle stages contributing to this impact category are the "pro- duction of biomass" and the "co-product utilization" in the case of anaerobic digestion plants. The land use originating from "transport" or "infrastructure" is negligible. For the plant types and substrates investigated, the production of wood chips (especially from round wood in the case of the

gasification plant) needs more land input (2.5 a-1 kWh than the production of energy crops (0.8 kWh-1). For the different types of anaerobic digestion plants, the share of the life cycle stage "production of biomass" increases with the share of energy crops in the mix of substrates. Especially for plants using a high share of organic residues, the need for additional agricultural land for the spreading of the digestate is greater. For the plant types investigated, the greatest land input is needed for the anaerobic digestion in small plants (AD, <50 kW, kWh and plants using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI, 2.9 (m ² a-1 kWh-1).
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the
Table 8 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of calculated emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel in %
Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.
