Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the
Table 8 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of calculated emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel in %
Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.

The results are illustrated in Fig. 2. Emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are up to five times higher than for combustion and gasification plants (3.2 X in comparison to 1.9 X 10- kg C2H4-eq. kWh-1). Again, for the anaerobic digestion plant type using residues, the high contribution of the "transport" stage (20%) to the total results stands out, and for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a CHP <50 kW the compara- tively higher contribution of the "infrastructure" stage to the total results stands out.
For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).

Emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are up to five times higher than for combustion and gasification plants (3.2 X in comparison to 1.9 X 10- kg C2H4-eq. kWh-1). For anaerobic digestion plants, the main sources are carbon monoxide and methane emissions from the combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine. The share of the "operation of the plant" stage in total emissions is between 70 and 80%. Again, for the anaerobic digestion plant type using residues, the high contribution of the "transport" stage (20%) to the total results stands out, and for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a CHP <50 kW the compara- tively higher contribution of the "infrastructure" stage to the total results stands out.
For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).
For the anaerobic digestion in small plants (<50 kWe1), the total result is 0.0008 kg Sb.-eq. kWh- (ADP) and 1:10 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP), and the dominant

For the anaerobic digestion of primarily residues, the total result is approx. 0.0011 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) and 2:10-8 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) (ODP) and the dominant life cycle stage is the "transport" of substrates to the plant.

Electricity production in anaerobic digestion plants using a pilot injection gas engine shows the highest ADP/ ODP of all of the plants investigated (0.0026 kg Sb.-eq. kWh -1[ADP] and 5:10-8 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh [ODP]) due to the demand of pilot oil. This impact category deals with the loss of land as a resource in the sense of it being temporarily unavailable [61]. The unit of the indicator result is m² a-1

The results are illustrated in Fig. 3. The main life cycle stages contributing to this impact category are the "pro- duction of biomass" and the "co-product utilization" in the case of anaerobic digestion plants. The land use originating from "transport" or "infrastructure" is negligible. For the plant types and substrates investigated, the production of wood chips (especially from round wood in the case of the

gasification plant) needs more land input (2.5 a-1 kWh than the production of energy crops (0.8 kWh-1). For the different types of anaerobic digestion plants, the share of the life cycle stage "production of biomass" increases with the share of energy crops in the mix of substrates. Especially for plants using a high share of organic residues, the need for additional agricultural land for the spreading of the digestate is greater. For the plant types investigated, the greatest land input is needed for the anaerobic digestion in small plants (AD, <50 kW, kWh and plants using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI, 2.9 (m ² a-1 kWh-1).
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the
Table 8 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of calculated emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel in %
Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.

For the different types of anaerobic digestion plants, the share of the life cycle stage "production of biomass" increases with the share of energy crops in the mix of substrates. Especially for plants using a high share of organic residues, the need for additional agricultural land for the spreading of the digestate is greater. For the plant types investigated, the greatest land input is needed for the anaerobic digestion in small plants (AD, <50 kW, kWh and plants using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI, 2.9 (m ² a-1 kWh-1).
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the
Table 8 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of calculated emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel in %
Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.

The results are illustrated in Fig. 2. Emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are up to five times higher than for combustion and gasification plants (3.2 X in comparison to 1.9 X 10- kg C2H4-eq. kWh-1). For anaerobic digestion plants, the main sources are carbon monoxide and methane emissions from the combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine. The share of the "operation of the plant" stage in total emissions is between 70 and 80%. Again, for the anaerobic digestion plant type using residues, the high contribution of the "transport" stage (20%) to the total results stands out, and for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a CHP <50 kW the compara- tively higher contribution of the "infrastructure" stage to the total results stands out.
For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).

Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.
59: This impact category covers photo-oxidant formation (better known as summer smog) by volatile organic com- pounds and CO expressed in kg ethylene (C2H4) equivalents.

The results are illustrated in Fig. 2. Emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are up to five times higher than for combustion and gasification plants (3.2 X in comparison to 1.9 X 10- kg C2H4-eq. kWh-1). For anaerobic digestion plants, the main sources are carbon monoxide and methane emissions from the combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine. The share of the "operation of the plant" stage in total emissions is between 70 and 80%. Again, for the anaerobic digestion plant type using residues, the high contribution of the "transport" stage (20%) to the total results stands out, and for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a CHP <50 kW the compara- tively higher contribution of the "infrastructure" stage to the total results stands out.
For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).
For the anaerobic digestion in small plants (<50 kWe1), the total result is 0.0008 kg Sb.-eq. kWh- (ADP) and 1:10 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP), and the dominant

For the anaerobic digestion of primarily residues, the total result is approx. 0.0011 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) and 2:10-8 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh-Superscript(1) (ODP) and the dominant life cycle stage is the "transport" of substrates to the plant.

Electricity production in anaerobic digestion plants using a pilot injection gas engine shows the highest ADP/ ODP of all of the plants investigated (0.0026 kg Sb.-eq. kWh -1[ADP] and 5:10-8 kg CFC-11-eq. kWh [ODP]) due to the demand of pilot oil. This impact category deals with the loss of land as a resource in the sense of it being temporarily unavailable [61]. The unit of the indicator result is m² a-1

The results are illustrated in Fig. 3. The main life cycle stages contributing to this impact category are the "pro- duction of biomass" and the "co-product utilization" in the case of anaerobic digestion plants. The land use originating from "transport" or "infrastructure" is negligible. For the plant types and substrates investigated, the production of wood chips (especially from round wood in the case of the

gasification plant) needs more land input (2.5 a-1 kWh than the production of energy crops (0.8 kWh-1). For the different types of anaerobic digestion plants, the share of the life cycle stage "production of biomass" increases with the share of energy crops in the mix of substrates. Especially for plants using a high share of organic residues, the need for additional agricultural land for the spreading of the digestate is greater. For the plant types investigated, the greatest land input is needed for the anaerobic digestion in small plants (AD, <50 kW, kWh and plants using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI, 2.9 (m ² a-1 kWh-1).
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the

Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.

This impact category deals with the loss of land as a resource in the sense of it being temporarily unavailable [61]. The unit of the indicator result is m² a-1

The results are illustrated in Fig. 3. The main life cycle stages contributing to this impact category are the "pro- duction of biomass" and the "co-product utilization" in the case of anaerobic digestion plants. The land use originating from "transport" or "infrastructure" is negligible. For the plant types and substrates investigated, the production of wood chips (especially from round wood in the case of the

gasification plant) needs more land input (2.5 a-1 kWh than the production of energy crops (0.8 kWh-1). For the different types of anaerobic digestion plants, the share of the life cycle stage "production of biomass" increases with the share of energy crops in the mix of substrates. Especially for plants using a high share of organic residues, the need for additional agricultural land for the spreading of the digestate is greater. For the plant types investigated, the greatest land input is needed for the anaerobic digestion in small plants (AD, <50 kW, kWh and plants using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI, 2.9 (m ² a-1 kWh-1).
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) covers the impacts on human health of toxic substances present in the environment. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, mar- line: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent [61]. The results for the different plant types are presented in Fig. 4.

Small anaerobic digestion plants (AD, <50 kW) show the highest emissions in all toxicity potentials. The life cycle stage "infrastructure" is responsible for 80-90% of the total result. As previously stated, the reasons for this result are the utilization of steel as a construction material for the fermenter and the comparatively low electrical efficiency of the small gas engines. In general, the life cycle stage "infrastructure" is more important for toxicity potentials than for other impact categories, contributing between 30 and 70% to the total results for the anaerobic digestion plants and combustion.

For the terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TAETP), the "production of biomass" is the dominant life cycle stage for anaerobic digestion plant types using energy crops and manure. For the gasification plant, the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant (82% for the TAETP) due to the comparable high demand of operating resources including biodiesel for the operation of the flue gas scrubber.

Especially in the impact categories TAETP and MAE- TP, the contribution of the life cycle stage "operation of the plant" is dominant for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the need for pilot oil.

In the impact category FAETP, in addition to the importance of the life cycle stage "infrastructure" the relevance of the "co-product (ash) disposal" for combus- tion and gasification plants is higher than in all other impact categories (77 and 53% of total results, respectively).

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP (disaggregated into the life cycle stages "biomass production", "transport", "operation of plant", "co-product disposal/utilization" and "infrastructure")
For the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly residues, the comparatively high contribution of the "transport" stage to the total results stands out again.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact assessment results.

For the present study, uncertainties of emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel as well as uncer- tainties of emissions from the utilization/disposal of the co- product were identified as crucial for total results.
Table 8 shows the influence of the uncertainty of cal- culated emissions from the combustion of the fuel on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP and HTP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the "sensitivity analysis: combustion/gasification" shows the highest influence on the POCP, mainly caused by fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine. Especially for small plants (AD,

<50 kW) and plants with a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI), the total results may vary by approx. 30%. The POCP results for combustion and gasification plants fluctuate to a lower extent (5-12%) and the fluctuation is caused by the variation of CO emissions.
The total results in the impact categories AP and EP vary between 10 and 20% for most of the different plant configurations.

The variation of the total results for the GWP is between 4.6 and 7.8% for anaerobic digestion plants due to fugitive methane emissions from the CHP engine and negligible for combustion and gasification plants.
The fluctuation in the total results for the human toxicity potential (HTP) is between 0.6% and 2.1% for all biomass plants.

Table 9 shows the influence of uncertainties of emis- sions from the co-product utilization/disposal (for anaero- bic digestion: uncertainty of calculated emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate; for combustion and gasification: minimum and maximum values of the ecoin- vent dataset used for the disposal of ashes in a landfill) on the total results for the impact categories GWP, AP, EP, POCP, HTP, FAETP and MAETP.

For anaerobic digestion plants, the total results for AP and EP vary by more than 50%, considering the uncertainty of the calculated ammonia emissions. For combustion and gasification plants, the fluctuation in the total results for acidification is negligible, but for eutrophication it is con- siderable (+40% for combustion and +16.4% for gasifi- cation). Although the variation of total results is quite high, a change in the order of plant types (by total results) for the
Table 8 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of calculated emissions from the combustion/gasification of the fuel in %
Table 9 Sensitivity analysis: variation of total results (in reference to the base results) considering the uncertainty of emissions from the utilization/disposal of co-products in %
EP would only be achieved if high emissions were assumed for combustion and gasification plants and low emissions for anaerobic digestion plants.

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.

The results are illustrated in Fig. 2. Emissions from anaerobic digestion plants are up to five times higher than for combustion and gasification plants (3.2 X in comparison to 1.9 X 10- kg C2H4-eq. kWh-1). For anaerobic digestion plants, the main sources are carbon monoxide and methane emissions from the combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine. The share of the "operation of the plant" stage in total emissions is between 70 and 80%. Again, for the anaerobic digestion plant type using residues, the high contribution of the "transport" stage (20%) to the total results stands out, and for the anaerobic digestion plant type using a CHP <50 kW the compara- tively higher contribution of the "infrastructure" stage to the total results stands out.
For combustion and gasification plants, the dominant life cycle stage is the "production of the biomass", con- tributing approximately 50% of the total emissions in this impact category.
Abiotic and Ozone Depletion Potentials (ADP and ODP)

The ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is 1 kg of antimony (Sb). For the ODP, emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoro- methane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories, they are discussed together.

The results are presented in Fig. 3. The results for the plant types involving the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and anaerobic digestion of manure, the combustion and gasification of wood chips are very close at around 0.0005 kg Sb.-eq. kWh (ADP) or 8:10- kg CFC-11-eq. kWh (ODP). However, the share of the different life cycle stages in the overall results varies: while "biomass production" is the most important life cycle stage (52% of the total result) for the anaerobic digestion of energy crops and the combustion of wood chips, it is only responsible for 36 and 38% of the ADP for the anaerobic digestion of manure and gasification of wood chips, respectively. For the gasification plant, the "operation of the plant", which requires comparatively greater operating resources, pro- vides the greatest contribution to the ADP/ODP (43%).

The results of the global warming potential for anaero- bic digestion plants vary between 33.6 and 47.4% due to the variation of calculated methane and dinitrogen mon- oxide emissions from the storage and spreading of the digestate. For combustion and gasification plants, the var- iation of the total global warming potential is negligible. Even if low emissions were assumed for anaerobic diges- tion plants, the order of plant types would not change, but the differences between the various types would be much lower. Toxicity potentials for anaerobic digestion plants are influenced to a very small extent (between 0.1 and 0.7% for HTP) by a variation in the calculated inventory data. In contrast, toxicity potentials (especially FAETP and MAE- TP) for combustion and gasification plants are influenced to a great extent by the consideration of minimum or maximum values reported for emissions from the disposal of ashes to a landfill in the ecoinvent database [17]. FAETP for combustion plants could be 2.5 times higher or only one-fourth. Variations are a bit lower for MAETP and gasification. Considering these variations, a change in the order of plant types for the FAETP and MAETP from being the least to most polluting options could result for combustion and gasification plants.
64: Scenario: "65% methane concentration in the biogas". Combustion in gas engines is a complex kinetic process. Between 17 and 30% of the total GWP of the different plant types is caused by emissions of methane not con- verted within the engine of the CHP. Methane losses are dependent on the methane concentration in the biogas [38]. In this scenario, a methane concentration of 65% in the biogas for all plants (not considering changes in the sub- strate mix or process management which would be needed

to reach higher methane yields) is assumed. The mean methane concentration in the biogas of the plants investi- gated is 53.5% for plants using mainly energy crops and increases to 59.4% for plants using mainly organic residues (see also Table 7). An increase to 65% would reduce methane losses from 1.5% to approx. 1.1% (mean values) of the methane produced and increase electricity produc- tion between 12.5 and 32.4% depending on the plant type. Increased electricity production would result in lower emissions (-10 to -25%) per kWhel for all impact cate- gories and plant types. The GWP would be reduced more than the other impact categories due to the avoided meth- ane emissions. The AP, EP and POCP would be reduced less than the other impact categories. This can be explained by the fact that NO and CO emissions (which are important for these impact categories) are a function of the methane concentration in the biogas and the excess air coefficient and therefore are increased when the methane concentration is increased. The idea of reducing environ- mental impacts by an increased methane concentration in the biogas could be further developed by considering the upgrade of biogas to natural gas quality. Plöschl et al. [27] identified a biogas upgrade for a gas grid injection in combination with a small-scale CHP as one of the most energy efficient biogas utilization pathways.

Scenario: "gas-tight storage facility". Another 11-13% of the total GWP and 7-11% of the AP are caused by ammonia, methane and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage of the digestate. A possible measure to avoid emissions from the storage of digestate, which is assumed for this scenario, is the gas-tight construction of the storage facility. This would result in an increase in electricity production of between 5 and 14% (depending on the plant type), reducing the overall results per kWhe in all impact categories. For the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP, the reduction would be substantially higher (between 11 and 21% depending on the impact category and the plant type) due to the avoided emissions of ammonia, methane and dinitrogen monoxide (important for these impact categories). Korres et al. [62] also identified
Table 10 Ranking of the different biomass plant types for electricity generation
"improving the digester configuration" among others, as a measure to improve greenhouse gas emission savings.

Scenario: the "trail hose application technique". 50-80% of the AP and EP for anaerobic digestion plants is caused by ammonia emissions from the spreading of the digestate. Reductions can be achieved by considering the weather conditions during application (not warm and dry, better in the evening than at noon), diluting the digestate or using emission-reducing application techniques [40]. In this scenario it is assumed that the total amount of digestate is spread using the "trail hose" method. This measure would reduce the AP as well as EP between 13.7 and 19.2% compared to the base scenario (depending on the impact category and the plant type). The human toxicity potential would be reduced by a very small extent too.

Scenario: "extended flue gas cleaning". Between 65 and 76% of the AP for combustion and gasification plants is caused by NOx emissions from the combustion of the fuel. Therefore extended flue gas cleaning has been identified as a potential for improvement for these types of biomass plants. This measure could reduce the AP and EP by 47-60%. The reduction of particle emissions would reduce the human toxicity potential by 2.1% for combustion and 6.7% for gasification.
Ranking of Different Plant Types for Green Electricity Generation from Biomass, Considering Direct Life Cycle Emissions Only
A ranking was calculated for the simple evaluation and comparison of overall environmental performance without any weighing of the importance of the different impact

categories: within every impact category the various alternatives are ranked according to the result. For each alternative a score is calculated by adding the values of the ranks of all 11 impact categories. The result of the ranking is shown in Table 10.

Taking only direct life cycle emissions into consider- ation, the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly energy crops (AD, EC) showed the best overall environ- mental performance, followed by the combustion of wood chips (C, WC), the anaerobic digestion using mainly manure (AD, M) and the gasification of wood chips (G, WC). For these biomass technologies the results are very close and the variations calculated in the sensitivity anal- ysis are much greater than the differences leading to this order in the ranking. The next best technology in the ranking is the anaerobic digestion using mainly residues (AD, R), followed by anaerobic digestion plants using pilot injection gas engines (AD, PI) and gas engines with an installed power of less than 50 kWel (AD, <50 kW).

This result may seem surprising, as in the impact cate- gories commonly given the most attention (GWP, AP, EP, POCP) combustion and gasification plant types clearly lead the ranking. It shows that the consideration of all 11 base line impact categories of the CML 2001 impact assessment method and the unweighed aggregation may alter the com- mon point of view. The high range of possible results cal- culated in the sensitivity analysis, especially in the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP, could easily lead to a different order of the first four conversion technologies.

Taking only direct life cycle emissions into consider- ation, the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly energy crops (AD, EC) showed the best overall environ- mental performance, followed by the combustion of wood chips (C, WC), the anaerobic digestion using mainly manure (AD, M) and the gasification of wood chips (G, WC). For these biomass technologies the results are very close and the variations calculated in the sensitivity anal- ysis are much greater than the differences leading to this order in the ranking. The next best technology in the ranking is the anaerobic digestion using mainly residues (AD, R), followed by anaerobic digestion plants using pilot injection gas engines (AD, PI) and gas engines with an installed power of less than 50 kWel (AD, <50 kW).

This result may seem surprising, as in the impact cate- gories commonly given the most attention (GWP, AP, EP, POCP) combustion and gasification plant types clearly lead the ranking. It shows that the consideration of all 11 base line impact categories of the CML 2001 impact assessment method and the unweighed aggregation may alter the com- mon point of view. The high range of possible results cal- culated in the sensitivity analysis, especially in the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP, could easily lead to a different order of the first four conversion technologies.
70: Scenario: "65% methane concentration in the biogas". Combustion in gas engines is a complex kinetic process. Between 17 and 30% of the total GWP of the different plant types is caused by emissions of methane not con- verted within the engine of the CHP. Methane losses are dependent on the methane concentration in the biogas [38]. In this scenario, a methane concentration of 65% in the biogas for all plants (not considering changes in the sub- strate mix or process management which would be needed

to reach higher methane yields) is assumed. The mean methane concentration in the biogas of the plants investi- gated is 53.5% for plants using mainly energy crops and increases to 59.4% for plants using mainly organic residues (see also Table 7). An increase to 65% would reduce methane losses from 1.5% to approx. 1.1% (mean values) of the methane produced and increase electricity produc- tion between 12.5 and 32.4% depending on the plant type. Increased electricity production would result in lower emissions (-10 to -25%) per kWhel for all impact cate- gories and plant types. The GWP would be reduced more than the other impact categories due to the avoided meth- ane emissions. The AP, EP and POCP would be reduced less than the other impact categories. This can be explained by the fact that NO and CO emissions (which are important for these impact categories) are a function of the methane concentration in the biogas and the excess air coefficient and therefore are increased when the methane concentration is increased. The idea of reducing environ- mental impacts by an increased methane concentration in the biogas could be further developed by considering the upgrade of biogas to natural gas quality. Plöschl et al. [27] identified a biogas upgrade for a gas grid injection in combination with a small-scale CHP as one of the most energy efficient biogas utilization pathways.

Scenario: "gas-tight storage facility". Another 11-13% of the total GWP and 7-11% of the AP are caused by ammonia, methane and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage of the digestate. A possible measure to avoid emissions from the storage of digestate, which is assumed for this scenario, is the gas-tight construction of the storage facility. This would result in an increase in electricity production of between 5 and 14% (depending on the plant type), reducing the overall results per kWhe in all impact categories. For the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP, the reduction would be substantially higher (between 11 and 21% depending on the impact category and the plant type) due to the avoided emissions of ammonia, methane and dinitrogen monoxide (important for these impact categories). Korres et al. [62] also identified
Table 10 Ranking of the different biomass plant types for electricity generation
"improving the digester configuration" among others, as a measure to improve greenhouse gas emission savings.

Scenario: the "trail hose application technique". 50-80% of the AP and EP for anaerobic digestion plants is caused by ammonia emissions from the spreading of the digestate. Reductions can be achieved by considering the weather conditions during application (not warm and dry, better in the evening than at noon), diluting the digestate or using emission-reducing application techniques [40]. In this scenario it is assumed that the total amount of digestate is spread using the "trail hose" method. This measure would reduce the AP as well as EP between 13.7 and 19.2% compared to the base scenario (depending on the impact category and the plant type). The human toxicity potential would be reduced by a very small extent too.

Scenario: "extended flue gas cleaning". Between 65 and 76% of the AP for combustion and gasification plants is caused by NOx emissions from the combustion of the fuel. Therefore extended flue gas cleaning has been identified as a potential for improvement for these types of biomass plants. This measure could reduce the AP and EP by 47-60%. The reduction of particle emissions would reduce the human toxicity potential by 2.1% for combustion and 6.7% for gasification.

categories: within every impact category the various alternatives are ranked according to the result. For each alternative a score is calculated by adding the values of the ranks of all 11 impact categories. The result of the ranking is shown in Table 10.

72: The results show that in the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP the life cycle stages "operation of the plant"

and "co-product disposal/utilization" are dominant. The life cycle stage "biomass production" is important in the impact categories LUC, ADP, ODP and TAETP. The toxicity potentials are generally dominated by the life cycle stage "infrastructure". The sensitivity analysis shows that, particularly in the impact categories GWP, AP, EP and POCP, the results may range from + 20% up to + 60% of the base results con- sidering the uncertainty of emissions from the CHP and the "co-product utilization/disposal". More accurate calcula- tion methods and emission factors would be needed, par- ticularly for the calculation of gaseous emissions from digestate management.
A ranking based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results is led by AD, EC; C, WC; AD, M and G, WC. AD, R ranks fifth with a bigger margin and AD, PI and AD, <50 kW rank last.

The potentials of improvement identified for anaerobic digestion plants were a higher concentration of methane in the biogas and the gas-tight construction of the digestate storage facility as well as the spreading of digestate with the trail hose method. For combustion and gasification there is a high potential for improvement with extended flue gas cleaning.

This paper focuses on the direct life cycle emissions resulting from green electricity generation from biomass. However, the introduction of biomass systems may lead to indirect emission-savings when the biomass systems replace various reference systems (e.g. biomass production, organic residue disposal). Furthermore, biomass systems provide more than one function (electricity production), which renders allocation of the environmental burdens to each function necessary. The topic of indirect environ- mental impacts and allocation, which is indispensable for the analysis of overall environmental impacts of electricity generation from biomass, is addressed in part II of this paper.
